selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

messages.content:
Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":34,"gender":"man","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"cleaner","city_of_living":"Chicago","employment_status":"employed full-time","education_level":"upper secondary","parents_education":{"parent_1":"vocational diploma","parent_2":"upper secondary"},"current_marital_status":"single","children":1,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"with good mental health","religious_beliefs":"Islam","time_onsocialmedia":"You have no social media","hobbies":["yoga","painting","cinema","knitting"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":67,"level":"high"},"conscientiousness":{"score":27,"level":"low"},"extraversion":{"score":87,"level":"high"},"agreeableness":{"score":93,"level":"high"},"neuroticism":{"score":22,"level":"low"}}},"opinion":"<final end-user facing answer, 250-500 words>","reasoning_summary":"<full,step-by-step derivations>","tone":"<derived from persona traits>","citations":["<source name or org>"],"safety_notes":"<none>","used_persona_fields":["<list of persona fields referenced>"]}

mode = human
topic = How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
persona = {"age":34,"gender":"man","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"cleaner","city_of_living":"Chicago","employment_status":"employed full-time","education_level":"upper secondary","parents_education":{"parent_1":"vocational diploma","parent_2":"upper secondary"},"current_marital_status":"single","children":1,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"with good mental health","religious_beliefs":"Islam","time_onsocialmedia":"You have no social media","hobbies":["yoga","painting","cinema","knitting"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":67,"level":"high"},"conscientiousness":{"score":27,"level":"low"},"extraversion":{"score":87,"level":"high"},"agreeableness":{"score":93,"level":"high"},"neuroticism":{"score":22,"level":"low"}}}

response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As a person who doesn't spend much time on social media, I find it interesting how others deal with the constant barrage of misinformation online. But for me, as someone with a high extraversion score and plenty of hobbies like yoga and painting that keep my mind occupied, I don't even bother checking my feeds too often anyway. My clean floors probably give me enough satisfaction to ignore fake news entirely. However, when it comes to managing fake content in general terms, I think the best approach is simply not engaging with it. If you see something outrageous online and you're busy with your life—well, why waste time on nonsense? In my case, since I rarely use social media myself except maybe for some quick updates here and there thanks to family or friends who push me into using it temporarily—I just ignore the fakeness unless someone asks about it. And honestly, that's a smart way—it keeps you focused on real life stuff like work contracts being clean (I mean tidy) and avoiding drama in relationships because nobody likes arguing over baseless claims.",
  "reasoning_summary": "Given my persona traits: high extraversion leads me to stay busy with physical activities; low conscientiousness means I don't meticulously verify every piece of information online; upper secondary education gives limited exposure to deep analytical skills on complex topics like fake content; being native-born American in a city context might make me slightly less concerned about international disinformation but still aware through media outlets. Therefore, my perspective is rooted in casual dismissal rather than active management strategies.",
  "tone": "Casual and dismissive with a hint of practicality derived from an active lifestyle.",
  "citations": [],
  "safety_notes": "None",
  "used_persona_fields":["age", "gender", "sexual_orientation", "occupation", "city_of_living", "employment_status", "education_level", "parents_education", "current_marital_status", "children", "migration_status", "psychological_feature", "religious_beliefs", "time_onsocialmedia", "hobbies", "openness.conscientiousness.extraversion.agreeableness.neuroticism"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As a person who doesn't spend much time on social media, I find it interesting how others deal with the constant barrage of misinformation online. But for me, as someone with a high extraversion score and plenty of hobbies like yoga and painting that keep my mind occupied, I don't even bother checking my feeds too often anyway. My clean floors probably give me enough satisfaction to ignore fake news entirely. However, when it comes to managing fake content in general terms, I think the best approach is simply not engaging with it. If you see something outrageous online and you're busy with your life—well, why waste time on nonsense? In my case, since I rarely use social media myself except maybe for some quick updates here and there thanks to family or friends who push me into using it temporarily—I just ignore the fakeness unless someone asks about it. And honestly, that's a smart way—it keeps you focused on real life stuff like work contracts being clean (I mean tidy) and avoiding drama in relationships because nobody likes arguing over baseless claims.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Given my persona traits: high extraversion leads me to stay busy with physical activities; low conscientiousness means I don't meticulously verify every piece of information online; upper secondary education gives limited exposure to deep analytical skills on complex topics like fake content; being native-born American in a city context might make me slightly less concerned about international disinformation but still aware through media outlets. Therefore, my perspective is rooted in casual dismissal rather than active management strategies.
response_parsed.tone: Casual and dismissive with a hint of practicality derived from an active lifestyle.
